elements.subsection-2411-001: Singular harmonic maps and renormalised energies
elements.sent-2411-004: Singular harmonic maps to the circle were first studied in the pioneering work of Bethuel, Brezis and H\'elein \cite{Bethuel-Brezis-Helein1994 in relation with Ginzburg--Landau vortices.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Even though the class of $W^{1,2 (\Omega,\s^1)$ maps with trace $\varphi\colon \partial\Omega\to\s^1$ is empty whenever $\deg(\varphi)\ne 0$, they showed that minimisers $(u_\epsilon)$ of the Ginzburg--Landau energy \begin{equation}\label{eq: GL} E_\epsilon(u)=\int_\Omega |\d u|^2+\frac1{\epsilon^{2}}(1-|u|^2)^2,\end{equation} on a simply connected planar domain $\Omega$, converge in the singular limit to a map $u\colon \Omega\to \s^1$, harmonic away from $|\deg(\varphi)|$ singularities and distributionally harmonic everywhere, thus emphasising the relevance of \eqref{eq: GL as a variational relaxation to the (non-variational) problem of finding harmonic maps with prescribed point singularities.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, they showed that maps with these properties exist for any prescribed singular set and boundary datum, under a topological compatibility condition.
elements.sent-2411-007: They call such maps canonical harmonic maps.
elements.sent-2411-008: The position of these singularities is mediated by an interaction energy $W$ between the points of the singular set --- called renormalised energy --- obtained by the desingularised limit \begin{equation}W=\lim_{\epsilon\to 0}\left(E_\epsilon(u_\epsilon)-2\pi|\deg(\varphi)||\log\epsilon |\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-009: The same energy appears also in the context of $p$-harmonic maps, with $p<2$, again as a desingularised limit of the energy of minimisers \cite{Hardt-Lin1995 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: This suggests that the renormalised energy is really a part of the (infinite) energy of singular $\s^1$-valued harmonic maps, that manifests only when the Dirichlet energy is relaxed in some way.
elements.sent-2411-011: There are several directions in which this result can be generalised.
elements.sent-2411-012: For instance, an analogous interaction energy was found for point singularities in higher codimension via $p$-harmonic relaxation \cite{Hardt-Lin-Wang1997 or for general target manifolds \cite{Monteil-Rodiac-VanSchaftingen2021,Monteil-Rodiac-VanSchaftingen2022,VanSchaftingen-VanVaerenbergh2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Ignat and Jerrard \cite{Ignat-Jerrard2021 showed the validity of a similar result for unimodular vector fields on a closed Riemannian surface with a finite number of prescribed singular points, detecting a very interesting interplay with the genus of the surface.}
elements.sent-2411-014: For higher dimensional singularities, this interaction is understood in the case of nearly parallel vortex filaments in the three dimensional Euclidean space $\R^3$ via Ginzburg--Landau \cite{DelPinoKowalczyk2008,ContrerasJerrard2017, Davila-delPino-Medina-Rodiac2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In this work, we advance this program with a twofold goal:.
elements.sent-2411-016: Give a complete classification of the set of $\s^1$-valued maps on a closed Riemannian manifold $(M^n,g)$ with prescribed (2-codimensional) singular set, classically harmonic away from the singularities and distributionally harmonic on the entire $M$.
elements.sent-2411-017: Describe the renormalised energy of the singular set of $\s^1$-valued maps with higher-dimensional singularities, and its role in three different relaxations of the Dirichlet energy.
elements.sent-2411-018: For instance, we establish that the interaction between two embedded, disjoint $C^{1,1}$ curves $\gamma_1,\gamma_2\subset \R^3$ is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq: interaction} \int_{\gamma_1}\int_{\gamma_2}\frac{\dot\gamma_1(x)\cdot \dot\gamma_2(y)}{|x-y|}\d\mathcal{H}^1(x)\d\mathcal{H}^1(y),\end{equation} which is consistent with the known formula for nearly parallel vortex filaments.

elements.subsection-2411-003: Area in higher codimension via singular limits: an outlook
elements.sent-2411-063: The connection between relaxations of harmonic maps and the area of the singular set has been long known.
elements.sent-2411-064: In the Ginzburg--Landau context, this connection has been explored, among many works, in \cite{Bethuel-Brezis-Helein1993, Bethuel-Brezis-Helein1994, Struwe1994, Jerrard-Soner2002CalcVar, Jerrard-Soner2002JFA, Alberti-Baldo-Orlandi2005,Mesaric2009,Stern2021, Colinet-Jerrard-Sternberg2021, DePhilippis-Pigati2024 via $\Gamma$-convergence and energy concentration.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Similar results have been achieved for $p$-harmonic maps, see the works \cite{Hardt-Lin1987,Hardt-Lin1995,Hardt-Lin-Wang1997,Stern2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: In the codimension one case, the Allen--Cahn model provides natural analogue of \eqref{eq: GL .}
elements.sent-2411-067: In the works of Guaraco and Gaspar--Guaraco \cite{Guaraco2018,Gaspar-Guaraco2018 this model was successfully employed for the construction of critical points of the area functional via min-max on the equation, rather than on the surface itself, building on the prior regularity theory of \cite{Hutchinson-Tonegawa2000,Tonegawa2005,Tonegawa-Wickramasekera2012 .}}
elements.sent-2411-068: This program was brought forward in three dimensions in \cite{Chodosh-Mantoulidis2020 , where the authors managed to perform a delicate passage to the limit $\eps\to 0$ and showed the validity of the multiplicity one conjecture, as well as reproving Yau's conjecture on the existence of infinitely many minimal surfaces in closed 3-manifolds.}
elements.sent-2411-069: A very promising alternative to the Allen--Cahn equation is given by nonlocal minimal surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-070: Initially introduced in \cite{Caffarelli-Roquejoffre-Savin2010 , those objects recently received a renewed interest in a program of classical minimal hypersurface approximation via nonlocal minmax \cite{Chan-Dipierro-Serra-Valdinoci2023, Caselli-Florit-Serra2024Yau,Florit2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-071: It seems only natural then to try to apply the same min-max scheme also in higher codimensional cases, for instance by means of Ginzburg--Landau or $p$-harmonic relaxation.
elements.sent-2411-072: The problem arising from this approach is twofold: first, the natural uniform bound on the energy is not enough to guarantee concentration into an integral varifold, as shown in \cite{Pigati-Stern2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Even worse, if the ambient manifold has nontrivial one-dimensional topology (e.g.~ $M=N\times\s^1$) then the min-max procedure could pick up solutions without any nodal/singular set, generating a diffuse limit, see \cite{Stern2020,Stern2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-074: An alternative approach is given by the Yang--Mills--Higgs equation, a modification of Ginzburg--Landau with features much closer to Allen--Cahn, which has proven to be a successful tool in the study of area in codimension two \cite{Riviere1996, Pigati-Stern2021, Parise-Pigati-Stern2024, Badran-DelPino2023, Badran-DelPino2024, MarxKuo2023, Liu-Wei-Ye2024, Liu-Ma-Wei-Wu2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Recently, Serra \cite{Serra2024 proposed another method, generalising the approximation by $s$-minimal hypersurfaces, based on a notion of fractional area in codimension $k\geq 2$.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Given a smooth, codimension $k$ submanifold $\Gamma$ and $s\in(0,1)$ we define its fractional mass as \begin{equation}\label{eq: fractional mass} \mathbb{M}^{n-k}_s(\Gamma)\coloneqq \inf\, [u]_{H^{(k-1+s)/2}(M)}^2\end{equation} where the infimum is taken over all maps $u\colon M\setminus\Gamma\to\s^{k-1}$ that are linked with $\Gamma$ and $H^{(k-1+s)/2 (M)$ is the fractional Sobolev space on a closed manifold $M$, introduced in \cite{Caselli-Florit-Serra2024 .}}

elements.sent-2411-413: In order to get a better picture of the self-interaction energy of the singular set, we give an explicit expression of renormalised energy in the Euclidean space.
elements.sent-2411-414: We start with the simplest case: the interaction energy between two embedded curves $\gamma_1,\gamma_2$ in $\R^3$.
elements.sent-2411-415: The fundamental solution $G(x,y)$ for the Hodge Laplacian on 1-forms is given by \begin{equation}G(x,y)=\sum_{j,k=1}^3\frac{\delta_{jk}}{4\pi|x-y|}\d x^j\wedge \d y^k\end{equation} where we remark that the sign follows by the fact that the Hodge Laplacian is the positive spectrum Laplacian.
elements.sent-2411-416: Supposing the curves parametrised by arclength, their orientation is given by \begin{equation}\vec\gamma_k=\sum_{j=1}^3(\dot\gamma_k)_j\d x^j,\quad k=1,2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-417: Then, we have \begin{equation}(G(x,y),\vec\gamma_1(x)\wedge\vec\gamma_2(y))_{g_{\R^3}}=\sum_{j,k=1}^3\frac{\delta_{jk}}{4\pi|x-y|}(\dot\gamma_1(x))_j(\dot\gamma_2(y))_k=\frac{\dot\gamma_1(x)\cdot\dot\gamma_2(y)}{4\pi|x-y|}\end{equation} where the dot product is the scalar product in $\R^3$.
elements.sent-2411-418: Thus, the interaction energy between $\gamma_1$ and $ \gamma_2$ is given by \begin{equation}\int_{\gamma_1}\int_{\gamma_2}\frac{\dot\gamma_1(x)\cdot\dot\gamma_2(y)}{4\pi|x-y|}\d\mathcal{H}^{1}(x)\d\mathcal{H}^{1}(y).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-419: Observe that the same formula holds if $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ are different portions of the same connected component, as the desingularisation of Lemma \ref{lem: decomposition w happens only on the diagonal.}
elements.sent-2411-420: In higher dimension, the interaction is similar.
elements.sent-2411-421: In $\R^n$, the fundamental solution for the Hodge Laplacian is \begin{equation}G(x,y)=\sum_{I\in\mathcal{I}_{n-2}}^3\frac{\d x^I\wedge \d y^I}{(n-2)\omega_n|x-y|^{n-2}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-422: If $\Sigma_1$ and $\Sigma_2$ are $(n-2)$-dimensional embedded manifolds in $\R^n$ with respective orientations given by \begin{equation}\vec{\Sigma}_k(x)=\sum_{J\in \mathcal{I}_{n-2}}\alpha^k_J(x)\d x^J\end{equation} the interaction becomes \begin{equation}\int_{\Sigma_1}\int_{\Sigma_2}\frac{ \langle\vec{\Sigma}_1(x),\vec{\Sigma}_2(y)\rangle}{(n-2)\omega_n|x-y|^{n-2}}\d\mathcal{H}^{n-2}(x)\d\mathcal{H}^{n-2}(y).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-423: Observe that the interaction between orthogonal parts of the submanifold vanishes, while it is maximised on parallel pieces, with a sign depending on the orientation.
elements.sent-2411-424: The renormalised energy appears to be also deeply related to the theory of electromagnetism; it corresponds to the energy stored in the magnetic field generated by currents running along wires.
elements.sent-2411-425: Precisely, let $\gamma_1,\dots,\gamma_m$ be $m$ closed wires embedded in $\R^3$ in which currents $I_1,\dots, I_m$ are flowing parallel to the orientations $\vec\gamma_1,\dots,\vec\gamma_m$.
elements.sent-2411-426: The total electric current of the system is given by \begin{equation}J=\sum_{j=1}^mI_j\llbracket\gamma_j\rrbracket.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-427: The magnetic field $B$ generated by $J$ obeys Maxwell's equations \begin{equation}\nabla\times B=J,\quad \nabla\cdot B=0\end{equation} or, in the language of forms (recalling that the magnetic field is identified with a 2-form $B\in\Omega^2(\R^n)$, \begin{equation}\d^*B=J,\quad \d B =0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-428: The second equation implies, by Poincar\'e Lemma, that $B=\d A$ for some 1-form $A$ and by the first equation \begin{equation}\d^*\d A=J.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-429: Exactly as in \eqref{eq: d*A harmonic we can show that $\d^*A$ is harmonic, thus $\d\d^*A=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-430: This means that \begin{equation}\Delta A=J\end{equation} which is the same equation satisfied by the $A$ in \S\ref{subs: ren en , up to modifying the definition to include the current intensities.}
elements.sent-2411-431: The magnetic energy is given by \begin{equation}\int|B|^2=\int|\d A|^2\end{equation} which is exactly the quantity computed in \eqref{eq: square norm singular term .}
elements.sent-2411-432: Thus, integrating in the complement of a $\delta$-tubular neighbourhood of the wires and after a $|\log\delta|$ renormalisation proportional to the length of the wires, we find the well known formula for the inductances \begin{equation}W_{\R^3}(\gamma_1,\dots,\gamma_m) =\sum_{j=1}^mI_j^2\int_{\gamma_j}\langle R_j(y),\vec\gamma_j(y)\rangle \dH^{n-2}(y) +\sum_{j\ne k}I_jI_k\int_{\gamma_j}\int_{\gamma_k}\frac{ \vec\gamma_j(x)\cdot \vec\gamma_k(y)}{|x-y|}\dH^{n-2}(x)\dH^{n-2}(y)\end{equation} where the first term is the sum of the (renormalised) self-inductances and the second term the mutual inductances of the wires, see \cite{Jackson1975 .. \bibliographystyle{aomalpha.}}
